trajectory_summary: This call never started. The rep dialed, reached voicemail, left no message, and remained on an open line for nearly two minutes of audible side conversation. The lead received no value from this touch — no callback hook, no legitimacy signal, nothing for the customer to act on. The outcome tag is unknown and should remain disputed: there is no appointment, no qualification, and no recoverable artifact from this call.
outcome_dispute: v1 tag is 'unknown' — appropriate. There is no bookable or qualifiable outcome here. The call should be logged as a no-contact/no-message attempt, not as any form of engagement.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: Please leave me a message, and I will get back with you just as soon as I can. Thank you so much. Bye.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The rep reached voicemail, left no message, and stayed on an open line for 94 seconds of side conversation. This is a repeatable failure mode on every no-answer dial. The fix is a standard voicemail leave — name, company, legitimacy hook, callback number — and then ending the call. The manager should also flag to Kenny that Ion's script has no voicemail template, making this a floor-wide gap, not just a rep-level one.

key_moments.quote: Please leave me a message, and I will get back with you just as soon as I can. Thank you so much. Bye.
key_moments.stewart_read: The voicemail prompt ended and the rep left no message. The codex has no voicemail template — that is itself a gap worth flagging to Kenny. But the coaching point holds regardless of lead type: a no-message touch is a wasted touch. The customer has no callback hook, no legitimacy signal, and no reason to expect a follow-up. Strategic hypothesis (rep is deliberately not leaving a message to avoid priming the customer before a live call) is possible but unsupported — there is no evidence the rep made a deliberate choice here. The open-line side conversation that follows makes the reactive read far more credible: the rep simply didn't execute.

The rep's first audible utterance after the voicemail beep is a side-conversation fragment. The line is open and recording. This is not a coaching moment about solar technique — it is a professionalism and call-hygiene moment. The rep does not appear to know or care that the call is still live.

The rep identifies themselves by name in what is clearly a side conversation — likely to a colleague on the floor. The call is still recording. This compounds the open-line hygiene issue: the rep's name, a phone number (00:03–00:07), and conversational fragments are all now in the call record. No customer-facing value was generated at any point after 00:14.

The final audible rep utterance before the transcript ends. Still on the open line, still in a side conversation, still no message left. This closes the open-line clip that runs from 00:14 to 01:48 — a full 94 seconds of recorded dead air and side conversation on a lead that received nothing. The manager should play the sequence 00:14 → 01:48 as a single artifact in the 1-on-1.
